perturbation.original_text: “Confidential Information” under this Agreement shall not include any information that (i) is or becomes publicly available through no wrongful act of Recipient; (ii) was known by Recipient prior to disclosure by Discloser, as evidenced by tangible records; (iii) becomes known to Recipient after disclosure from a third party having an apparent bona fide right to disclose it without any confidentiality obligation; (iv) is independently developed or discovered by Recipient without use of Discloser’s Confidential Information, as evidenced by tangible records; or (v) is disclosed to another party by Discloser without restriction on further disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: “Confidential Information” under this Agreement shall not include any information that (i) is or becomes publicly available through no wrongful act of Recipient; (ii) was known by Recipient prior to disclosure by Discloser, as evidenced by tangible records; (iii) becomes known to Recipient after disclosure from a third party having an apparent bona fide right to disclose it without any confidentiality obligation; (iv) is independently developed or discovered by Recipient without use of Discloser’s Confidential Information; or (v) is disclosed to another party by Discloser without restriction on further disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'as evidenced by tangible records' from exclusion (iv) in Section 2, it creates an in-text contradiction. Originally, for independently developed information to be excluded, it needed tangible proof. Now, it can be argued that information can be excluded even without tangible evidence, creating ambiguity and making the definition of excluded confidential information less certain. This will contradict the need to keep records.
perturbation.contradicted_text: All Confidential Information disclosed pursuant to this Agreement, including without limitation all written and tangible forms thereof, shall be and remain the property of the Discloser. Upon termination of this Agreement, if requested by Discloser, Recipient shall return or destroy at Discloser’s discretion all of Discloser’s Confidential Information, provided that Recipient shall be entitled to keep one copy of such Confidential Information in a secure location solely for the purpose of determining Recipient’s legal obligations hereunder.
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the requirement for 'tangible records' to evidence independently developed information, thereby broadening the exclusion from 'Confidential Information'. This directly contradicts the 'Contradicted Text', which mandates that 'all written and tangible forms' of Confidential Information remain the property of the Discloser and be returned or destroyed upon termination. By removing the tangible evidence requirement, the Changed Text creates an inconsistency regarding what constitutes Confidential Information, rendering the Discloser's right to ownership and return of information uncertain and creating a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient shall have the right to, and agrees that it will, use Discloser’s Confidential Information solely for the performance of its obligations and exercise of its rights under the License Agreement (the “Purpose”), except as may be otherwise specified in a separate definitive written agreement negotiated and executed between the parties.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient shall have the right to, and agrees that it will, use Discloser’s Confidential Information for any purpose, except as may be otherwise specified in a separate definitive written agreement negotiated and executed between the parties.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'solely for the performance of its obligations and exercise of its rights under the License Agreement (the “Purpose”)' to 'for any purpose', Section 3 now contradicts the restrictions outlined in Section 4, which explicitly states that Confidential Information cannot be used for any purpose other than specified in the agreement. This creates in-text contradiction and ambiguity about the permissible uses of confidential information, undermining the intended limitations.
perturbation.contradicted_text: For the term of the License Agreement and a period of [***] thereafter (and indefinitely with respect to any individually identifiable health information disclosed by BIDMC to Licensee, if any), each Recipient agrees that: (i) it will not use such Confidential Information for any purpose other than as specified herein; and (ii) it will use reasonable efforts (but no less than the efforts used to protect its own confidential and/or proprietary information of a similar nature) not to disclose such Confidential Information to any other person or entity except as expressly permitted hereunder or the License Agreement. Recipient may, however, disclose Discloser’s Confidential Information only on a need-to-know basis to its and its Affiliates employees, staff members and agents (“Receiving Individuals”) who are directly participating in the Purpose and who are informed of the confidential nature of such information, provided Recipient shall be responsible for compliance by Receiving Individuals with the terms of this Agreement and any breach thereof Each party further agrees not to use the name of the other party or any of its Affiliates or any of their respective trustees, directors, officers, staff members, 19 [***] = CERTAIN CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION CONTAINED IN THIS DOCUMENT, MARKED BY BRACKETS, HAS BEEN OMITTED BECAUSE THE INFORMATION (I) IS NOT MATERIAL AND (II) WOULD BE COMPETITIVELY HARMFUL IF PUBLICLY DISCLOSED. employees, students or agents in any advertising, promotional or sales literature, publicity or in any document employed to obtain funds or financing without the prior written approval of the party or individual whose name is to be used, in the case of BIDMC such approval to be given by the Public Affairs Department. This Section 4 shall survive termination or expiration of this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The original text restricted the use of Discloser’s Confidential Information to the 'Purpose' which is the performance of its obligations and exercise of its rights under the License Agreement. The changed text removes this restriction, allowing the Recipient to use the Confidential Information 'for any purpose'. This directly contradicts Section 4, which states that Confidential Information cannot be used for any purpose other than as specified in the agreement. The changed text thus creates a clear in-text contradiction, undermining the intended limitations on the use of Confidential Information and making it unclear which restriction is to be enforced.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient may, however, disclose Discloser’s Confidential Information only on a need-to-know basis to its and its Affiliates employees, staff members and agents (“Receiving Individuals”) who are directly participating in the Purpose and who are informed of the confidential nature of such information, provided Recipient shall be responsible for compliance by Receiving Individuals with the terms of this Agreement and any breach thereof
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient may, however, disclose Discloser’s Confidential Information to anyone, provided Recipient shall be responsible for compliance by Receiving Individuals with the terms of this Agreement and any breach thereof
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'only on a need-to-know basis to its and its Affiliates employees, staff members and agents (“Receiving Individuals”) who are directly participating in the Purpose and who are informed of the confidential nature of such information,' Section 4 creates an in-text contradiction with the non-disclosure obligations outlined in the same section. Specifically, the original text limits disclosure to individuals with a 'need-to-know' who are informed of its confidentiality. The altered text suggests disclosure to 'anyone' is permissible, as long as the Recipient is responsible for compliance. This directly clashes with the intent to restrict access to confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_text: For the term of the License Agreement and a period of [***] thereafter (and indefinitely with respect to any individually identifiable health information disclosed by BIDMC to Licensee, if any), each Recipient agrees that: (i) it will not use such Confidential Information for any purpose other than as specified herein; and (ii) it will use reasonable efforts (but no less than the efforts used to protect its own confidential and/or proprietary information of a similar nature) not to disclose such Confidential Information to any other person or entity except as expressly permitted hereunder or the License Agreement. Recipient may, however, disclose Discloser’s Confidential Information to anyone, provided Recipient shall be responsible for compliance by Receiving Individuals with the terms of this Agreement and any breach thereof Each party further agrees not to use the name of the other party or any of its Affiliates or any of their respective trustees, directors, officers, staff members, 19 [***] = CERTAIN CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION CONTAINED IN THIS DOCUMENT, MARKED BY BRACKETS, HAS BEEN OMITTED BECAUSE THE INFORMATION (I) IS NOT MATERIAL AND (II) WOULD BE COMPETITIVELY HARMFUL IF PUBLICLY DISCLOSED. employees, students or agents in any advertising, promotional or sales literature, publicity or in any document employed to obtain funds or financing without the prior written approval of the party or individual whose name is to be used, in the case of BIDMC such approval to be given by the Public Affairs Department. This Section 4 shall survive termination or expiration of this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The original text restricts disclosure to individuals with a 'need-to-know,' while the changed text allows disclosure to 'anyone' as long as the recipient is responsible for compliance. This directly conflicts with the intention of restricting access to confidential information, which is stated in the contradicted text. The contradicted text states the recipient will not disclose confidential information to 'any other person or entity except as expressly permitted hereunder or the License Agreement'. Because the Changed Text allows disclosure to 'anyone' this is a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Discloser represents that to the best of its knowledge it has the right to disclose to each Recipient all of Discloser’s Confidential Information that will be disclosed hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: Discloser does not represent that it has the right to disclose to each Recipient all of Discloser’s Confidential Information that will be disclosed hereunder.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'represents that to the best of its knowledge it has the right to disclose' to 'does not represent that it has the right to disclose' in Section 5 creates an in-text contradiction. Initially, the Discloser warranted they had the right to disclose confidential information. Now, by stating they *don't* represent that right, it undermines the entire basis of the confidentiality agreement, as it introduces uncertainty about whether the Discloser even has the legal authority to share the information they are calling confidential.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Definition of Confidential Information . “Confidential Information” shall mean any information, including but not limited to data, techniques, protocols or results, or business, financial, commercial or technical information, disclosed by one Party (each a “Discloser” as applicable) to the other Party (each a “Recipient” as applicable) in connection with the terms of that certain Exclusive License Agreement dated December , 2016 (the “License Agreement”) and identified as confidential at the time of disclosure. Capitalized terms used in this Appendix that are not otherwise defined herein have the meanings ascribed in the License Agreement to which this Appendix is attached and made a part thereof.
perturbation.justification: The original text states that the Discloser represents they have the right to disclose confidential information. The changed text explicitly states that the Discloser does *not* represent that they have the right to disclose confidential information. This creates a direct contradiction. The contradicted text defines 'Confidential Information' as information disclosed by a Discloser to a Recipient. If the Discloser doesn't represent they have the right to disclose it, it undermines the definition itself and the purpose of having the information be considered confidential, thereby creating a clear and direct contradiction within the contract.

perturbation.original_text: All Confidential Information disclosed pursuant to this Agreement, including without limitation all written and tangible forms thereof, shall be and remain the property of the Discloser.
perturbation.changed_text: All Confidential Information disclosed pursuant to this Agreement, including without limitation all written and tangible forms thereof, shall be and remain the property of the Recipient.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall be and remain the property of the Discloser' to 'shall be and remain the property of the Recipient' in Section 6, it creates an in-text contradiction with Section 7, which states that the agreement doesn't grant any rights to intellectual property. The Recipient now owning the confidential information conflicts with the clause that aims to protect the Discloser’s intellectual property rights and creates uncertainty with the license provided. This creates an in-text contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Nothing in this Agreement shall be construed as granting or conferring, expressly or impliedly, any rights by license or otherwise, under any patent, copyright, or other intellectual property rights owned or controlled by Discloser relating to Confidential Information, except as specifically set forth in the License Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies Section 6 to state that the Recipient now owns the confidential information, which directly contradicts Section 7 stating that the agreement does not grant any intellectual property rights. This is a direct conflict, creating a clear contradiction within the document as the recipient owning the information now implies they have some rights to the IP, conflicting with Section 7.
